nuance: Concedes a fact, then contradicts the expectation it sets up — bookish, a step above けど.

examples.en: Although I started speaking in Japanese, the words wouldn't come out.
Although I got the informal job offer, I still haven't decided on a place to live.
Although I submitted the documents, there's been no word about an interview.

caution: Unlike casual けど, ものの needs a genuine contradiction — you can't use it to simply link two neutral facts.

peg.ja: 強くなったものの、心はあの頃のままだ。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoku natta mono no, kokoro wa ano koro no mama da.
peg.en: I've grown stronger, but my heart remains as it was back then.
peg.source: 葬送のフリーレン — フリーレン

connection: V-stem / adj / N + ながらも
nuance: Two things hold at once against expectation — 'even while being X, still Y'.

examples.en: It's a small but cozy room.
Despite the heavy overtime, I study Japanese every day.
Foreigner though I am, I got used to the Japanese workplace quickly.

caution: Don't confuse concessive ながらも with simultaneous ながら — here it means "even though," not "while doing."

peg.ja: 小さいながらも、その刃は鬼を斬る。
peg.romaji: Chiisai nagara mo, sono yaiba wa oni o kiru.
peg.en: Small though it is, that blade can cut down a demon.
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 — 冨岡義勇

nuance: Literary 'while' — two simultaneous actions by one subject, stiffer than 〜ながら.

examples.en: I drank my morning coffee while gazing at the cherry blossoms outside the window.
I walked through the alleys while checking the map.
Considering my future, I keep on with my current job.

caution: つつ (simultaneous "while") and つつある (ongoing change) are different — plain つつ never means "in the process of."

peg.ja: 過ぎ去った日々を思い出しつつ、旅を続ける。
peg.romaji: Sugisatta hibi o omoidashi tsutsu, tabi o tsuzukeru.
peg.en: Recalling the days gone by, I continue my journey.
peg.source: 葬送のフリーレン — フリーレン

nuance: A change steadily underway toward completion — 'is coming to X', more formal than 〜ている.

examples.en: My Japanese is gradually improving, little by little.
It seems the rainy season is coming to an end.
Rents in the city center are steadily rising.

caution: Only verbs of gradual change take つつある; a momentary or stative verb (知る, ある) sounds wrong here.

peg.ja: 戦況は、静かに変わりつつある。
peg.romaji: Senkyō wa, shizuka ni kawari tsutsu aru.
peg.en: The tide of battle is quietly beginning to turn.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

nuance: Grants a hypothetical for argument's sake — 'even if we suppose X, still Y'.

examples.en: Even supposing I miss the last train, I can walk home.
Even supposing the rent is high, I want to live near this station.
Even supposing I'm hired, I don't know the work location yet.

caution: Don't confuse hypothetical としても ("even supposing") with unexpected-result にしては ("for a…"); としても grants a supposition, not a fact.

peg.ja: たとえ負けるとしても、退くつもりはない。
peg.romaji: Tatoe makeru to shite mo, shirizoku tsumori wa nai.
peg.en: Even supposing I lose, I have no intention of retreating.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — エレン

connection: N / plain form + にしても
nuance: Grants a point but pushes back — 'even taking X into account, it's too much / still Y'.

examples.en: Even considering it's my first business trip, I'm way underprepared.
Even considering it gets crowded, the morning Yamanote Line is brutal.
Even considering it's cheap, this room is too far from the station.

caution: にしても (grant a point, but…) vs にしては (surprising for…) are constantly swapped — にしても concedes, にしては contrasts with an implied standard.

peg.ja: 冗談にしても、笑えないな。
peg.romaji: Jōdan ni shite mo, waraenai na.
peg.en: Even taking it as a joke, that's not funny.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 — 五条悟

connection: N / plain form + にしては
nuance: Result clashes with the standard the topic implies — 'for an X, surprisingly Y'.

examples.en: I was told my keigo is good for a foreigner.
It was a cool day, considering it's June.
For a newcomer, he's remarkably calm.

caution: にしては needs a concrete yardstick from the topic; use にしても instead when granting a hypothetical rather than measuring against a standard.

peg.ja: 新入りにしては、いい動きだ。
peg.romaji: Shiniri ni shite wa, ii ugoki da.
peg.en: For a rookie, that's good movement.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! — 及川徹

nuance: Given X is now settled, a firm resolve or obligation follows — 'now that…, of course Y'.

examples.en: Now that I've come to Tokyo, I want to see all the festivals.
So long as I've taken it on, I'll see it through to the end.
Now that I'm working in Japan, I want to learn keigo properly.

caution: からには must be followed by resolve, obligation, or advice (〜べきだ, 〜つもりだ) — not a plain factual statement like から.

peg.ja: 海賊王を目指すからには、この海を越えていく。
peg.romaji: Kaizoku-ō o mezasu kara ni wa, kono umi o koete iku.
peg.en: Now that I aim to be Pirate King, I'll cross this sea.
peg.source: ワンピース — ルフィ

nuance: Pins a bad outcome on one small cause — 'just because of X, unfortunately Y'.

examples.en: Simply because I took the wrong road, I ended up late for the interview.
Simply because I misread the kanji, I got off at the wrong station.
Simply because I had no guarantor, I couldn't sign the contract.

caution: ばかりに is regret-only: the result must be negative/unwanted, so never use it for a happy outcome the way plain から allows.

peg.ja: 一瞬ためらったばかりに、仲間を失った。
peg.romaji: Isshun tameratta bakari ni, nakama o ushinatta.
peg.en: Simply because I hesitated for an instant, I lost a comrade.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — エドワード

connection: plain form / N + ばかりか
nuance: Escalates past the first item to a stronger one — 'not just X, but even Y on top'.

examples.en: The store manager remembered not only my name but even my hometown.
This share house is not only cheap but even close to the station.
Not only did the trains stop for the typhoon, even the buses were suspended.

caution: Don't confuse ばかりか ("not only… but even") with ばかりに ("simply because"); ばかりか adds, ばかりに blames.

peg.ja: 逃げ出さないばかりか、あいつは前に出た。
peg.romaji: Nigedasanai bakari ka, aitsu wa mae ni deta.
peg.en: Not only did he not flee, he even stepped forward.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — オールマイト

connection: N / plain form + のみならず
nuance: Formal, written 'not only X but also Y' — the bookish sibling of 〜だけでなく.

examples.en: This scheme concerns not only international students but also workers.
Tsukiji is popular not only with tourists but also with locals.
This system affects not only urban areas but also rural regions.

caution: のみならず is stiff written Japanese — using it in casual speech where だけでなく belongs sounds unnaturally bookish.

peg.ja: この力は自分のみならず、世界のために使う。
peg.romaji: Kono chikara wa jibun nomi narazu, sekai no tame ni tsukau.
peg.en: This power I'll use not only for myself but for the world.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

nuance: Piles a same-direction second point onto the first — 'not only that, and also'.

examples.en: On top of a long overtime shift today, I also missed the last train.
This room gets good sunlight, and on top of that it's quiet.
This job posting pays well, and on top of that it's near the station.

caution: The second clause of 上に must run the same direction as the first (both good or both bad) — mixing a plus and a minus needs 一方 or が instead.

peg.ja: 傷を負った上に、道まで見失った。
peg.romaji: Kizu o otta ue ni, michi made miushinatta.
peg.en: On top of being wounded, I even lost my way.
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 — 竈門炭治郎

nuance: Do the second thing on the basis of having first done X — a deliberate, ordered step.

examples.en: I want to decide on the contract only after viewing the place.
I'll go to the ward office after I've checked the documents.
I intend to apply only after carefully checking the conditions.

caution: 上で ("after doing, then…") requires V-た or N-の; attaching it to a plain non-past verb, or confusing it with additive 上に, is the classic slip.

peg.ja: すべてを見極めた上で、私は動く。
peg.romaji: Subete o mikiwameta ue de, watashi wa ugoku.
peg.en: Only after discerning everything do I act.
peg.source: デスノート — 夜神月

nuance: Sets two contrasting facts side by side — 'X, while on the other hand Y'.

examples.en: The city center is convenient, while on the other hand the rent is high.
My Japanese conversation has improved, whereas kanji is still my weak point.
Job openings keep increasing, while on the other hand applicants are decreasing.

caution: Contrastive 一方(で) links two opposing facts; don't confuse it with 一方だ ("keeps on…"), which describes a one-way trend.

peg.ja: 力を得た一方で、大切なものを失った。
peg.romaji: Chikara o eta ippō de, taisetsu na mono o ushinatta.
peg.en: I gained power, while on the other hand I lost what mattered.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — エドワード

nuance: A trend moving in one direction only, usually worsening — 'just keeps getting more X'.

examples.en: As summer approaches, the temperature just keeps on rising.
My savings just keep dwindling, so I'd better start working soon.
My eating-out costs just keep increasing.

caution: 一方だ attaches to a dictionary-form verb of change and means a worsening one-way trend — not the contrastive 一方で ("on the other hand").

peg.ja: 敵の数は、増える一方だ。
peg.romaji: Teki no kazu wa, fueru ippō da.
peg.en: The enemy's numbers just keep on increasing.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — アルミン

nuance: Two opposite sides of one thing — a pro paired with its con.

examples.en: The night shift pays well, but on the other side it's hard on the body.
City life has plenty of stimulation, while at the same time it's tiring.
This job pays well, but on the other side it has few days off.

caution: 反面 pairs two opposite sides of the SAME thing (a pro and its con); use 一方 when contrasting two different subjects.

peg.ja: その力は頼もしい反面、危うさもある。
peg.romaji: Sono chikara wa tanomoshii hanmen, ayausa mo aru.
peg.en: That power is dependable, but on the other side it's dangerous.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 — 五条悟

nuance: Two things coincide, or one triggers the other the instant it happens.

examples.en: The moment the doors opened, the passengers got off.
At the same time as joining the company, I rented a new place.
At the same time as signing the contract, I received the keys.

caution: と同時に needs true simultaneity or instant triggering; for "right after" a completed action, たとたん or てすぐ fits better.

peg.ja: 鐘が鳴ると同時に、決着はついた。
peg.romaji: Kane ga naru to dōji ni, kecchaku wa tsuita.
peg.en: The instant the bell rang, it was settled.
peg.source: ジョジョの奇妙な冒険 — 空条承太郎

nuance: Just as you registered X, the unexpected Y already followed — often surprise or reversal.

examples.en: No sooner had it cleared up than it started raining again.
No sooner had he come home than he left for work again.
No sooner did I think a train had come than it turned out to be a pass-through train.

caution: かと思ったら attaches to V-た and describes something already observed, so it can't take a first-person will or command in the second clause.

peg.ja: 消えたかと思ったら、もう背後にいた。
peg.romaji: Kieta ka to omottara, mō haigo ni ita.
peg.en: No sooner had he vanished than he was already behind me.
peg.source: NARUTO -ナルト- — はたけカカシ

nuance: Do the next thing the instant X is done — formal, common in business notices.

examples.en: I'll get in touch as soon as the interview result comes in.
I can move in as soon as a room opens up.
As soon as the materials arrive, I'll send them by email.

caution: 次第 ("as soon as") takes a V-stem and never a past tense, and no たら/ば conditional follows — don't confuse it with N+次第で ("depending on").

peg.ja: 準備が整い次第、作戦を開始する。
peg.romaji: Junbi ga totonoi shidai, sakusen o kaishi suru.
peg.en: As soon as preparations are ready, we begin the operation.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — エルヴィン

nuance: The outcome hinges entirely on X — 'it all depends on X'.

examples.en: I'll decide the weekend plans depending on the weather.
Depending on your effort, your Japanese can improve without limit.
Depending on the interview result, I can start working next month.

caution: 次第で ("depending on") attaches to a NOUN, while bare 次第 ("as soon as") attaches to a V-stem — swapping the attachment is the classic error.

peg.ja: お前の覚悟次第で、結末は変わる。
peg.romaji: Omae no kakugo shidai de, ketsumatsu wa kawaru.
peg.en: Depending on your resolve, the ending changes.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

nuance: Cites an extreme case to imply the rest — literary, often paired with a negative.

examples.en: When I'd just arrived, I couldn't even say my own address.
I'm so busy I don't even have time for lunch.
Having just moved in, I don't even know the local shops.

caution: すら is literary and marks an extreme case; it isn't interchangeable with additive さえ in conditional さえ〜ば ("if only").

peg.ja: 名前すら、思い出せなかった。
peg.romaji: Namae sura, omoidasenakatta.
peg.en: I couldn't even recall my own name.
peg.source: 東京喰種 — 金木研

nuance: A wish framed as barely-possible — 'if it were even possible to X (which it isn't really)'.

examples.en: If only I could go back, I'd return to the day I first arrived in Tokyo.
If only I could, I'd go to Hokkaido right now.
If I could, I'd live in this old-town district forever.

caution: This ものなら takes a potential verb ("if you could…"); don't confuse it with ようものなら ("if you were to…, dire result"), which takes volitional + もの.

peg.ja: 戻れるものなら、あの日に戻りたい。
peg.romaji: Modoreru mono nara, ano hi ni modoritai.
peg.en: If only I could go back, I'd return to that day.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — アルフォンス

nuance: The result holds firm so long as X does not happen — 'unless X, Y stays true'.

examples.en: Unless a typhoon comes, the fireworks festival will be held.
Unless I have a guarantor, I can't rent this room.
Unless you make a reservation, you can't get into this restaurant.

caution: ない限り means "unless (X stays false)" — remember the negative is built in, so don't add another "not" and accidentally double-negate.

peg.ja: 諦めない限り、負けじゃない。
peg.romaji: Akiramenai kagiri, make ja nai.
peg.en: Unless you give up, it's not a loss.
peg.source: スラムダンク — 安西先生

nuance: So long as the stated condition continues, the result holds — a standing boundary.

examples.en: As long as I live in Tokyo, my train-based lifestyle will continue.
As long as I'm healthy, I want to keep traveling while I work.
As long as I have a visa, I can keep working in Japan.

caution: 限り has several senses; here it means "as long as (a condition holds)," distinct from ない限り ("unless") and からには ("now that").

peg.ja: 命ある限り、俺は戦う。
peg.romaji: Inochi aru kagiri, ore wa tatakau.
peg.en: As long as I have life, I'll fight.
peg.source: ブリーチ — 黒崎一護

connection: N / plain form + だけあって
nuance: The result fits the reputation or status — 'befitting X, no wonder Y'. Admiring.

examples.en: As expected of a popular restaurant, the queue is huge.
As might be expected after studying every day, I passed the exam.
As expected of a popular share house, it filled up right away.

caution: だけあって is praise-only (an admirable, fitting result); for a negative or merely-natural consequence use だけに instead.

peg.ja: 隊長だけあって、判断が速い。
peg.romaji: Taichō dake atte, handan ga hayai.
peg.en: As expected of a captain, his judgment is fast.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — ジャン

nuance: The very fact of X makes the result stronger or more natural — 'being X, all the more Y'.

examples.en: Precisely because I'd been looking forward to it, the cancellation was a letdown.
Precisely because it was my first winter, Tokyo's cold really got to me.
Precisely because it was my first interview, I was very nervous.

caution: Unlike praise-only だけあって, だけに accepts negative results too, and often adds 余計に/なおさら ("all the more") — don't treat the two as identical.

peg.ja: 期待していただけに、その裏切りは応えた。
peg.romaji: Kitai shite ita dake ni, sono uragiri wa kotaeta.
peg.en: Precisely because I'd trusted him, that betrayal hit hard.
peg.source: 東京喰種 — 金木研

connection: N + から + N + にかけて
nuance: Marks the far end of a rough span (from A through B) — the range is approximate, not a precise deadline.

examples.en: From June into July, the rainy season keeps going.
From Friday into Sunday this week, heavy snow is forecast in Tokyo.
From December into January, the town glitters with illuminations.

caution: にかけて pairs with から to mark a vague span (A through B); don't confuse it with にかけては ("when it comes to, no one beats…"), a different pattern.

peg.ja: 夕方から夜にかけて、奴らは動き出す。
peg.romaji: Yūgata kara yoru ni kakete, yatsura wa ugokidasu.
peg.en: From evening into night, they make their move.
peg.source: デスノート — L

nuance: Stresses the whole extent of a large range — time, area, or scope. Formal; the noun names the span (three years, the whole country).

examples.en: I worked in Tokyo over the span of three years.
The typhoon's effects spread across a wide area.
The construction continued over the span of three months.

caution: にわたって stresses the whole extent of a span; don't confuse it with を通じて — わたって needs a noun that literally names a range (三年, 全国).

peg.ja: 十年にわたって、俺はこの日を待っていた。
peg.romaji: Jū-nen ni watatte, ore wa kono hi o matte ita.
peg.en: Over ten years, I've awaited this day.
peg.source: ジョジョの奇妙な冒険 — DIO

nuance: Two senses: a means/intermediary (via an app, through a friend) or a whole period/extent (throughout the year).

examples.en: Through a friend's introduction, I found my current share house.
Throughout the whole year, Tokyo has many tourists.
Through an app, I keep in touch with my work colleagues.

caution: を通じて has a "means" sense and a "throughout a period" sense; を通して is near-identical but leans toward the deliberate-means reading.

peg.ja: 痛みを通じて、人は強くなる。
peg.romaji: Itami o tsūjite, hito wa tsuyoku naru.
peg.en: Through pain, a person grows stronger.
peg.source: NARUTO -ナルト- — 自来也

nuance: Makes the noun the focal point around which the rest is organized — activity, movement, or discussion revolves around it.

examples.en: Centered on Shibuya, shops for young people are clustered together.
In the meeting, we talked mainly about next year's budget.
I'm looking for a room centered on the Shinjuku area.

caution: を中心に marks the focal point things revolve around (東京を中心に広がる); for a literal physical position at the center, の中心で is the usual choice — a tendency, not a hard rule.

peg.ja: この街を中心に、事件が起きている。
peg.romaji: Kono machi o chūshin ni, jiken ga okite iru.
peg.en: Centered on this town, incidents are occurring.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン — 江戸川コナン

nuance: Names one prominent representative example, implying many more of the same kind follow.

examples.en: Starting with Asakusa, Tokyo has many famous tourist spots.
Led by the store manager, all the staff are kind.
Starting with the résumé, I gathered the necessary documents.

caution: Don't shorten it to はじめ alone — the pattern needs を (Nをはじめ); をはじめとして is the same meaning in a stiffer, more formal coat, not a different grammar point.

peg.ja: 隊長をはじめ、全員が覚悟を決めていた。
peg.romaji: Taichō o hajime, zen'in ga kakugo o kimete ita.
peg.en: Starting with the captain, everyone had steeled themselves.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — アルミン

nuance: A stiffer, more written 〜について — good for topics of inquiry, reports, and formal statements.

examples.en: I asked at the ward office regarding the renewal of my residence card.
If you have questions regarding the new contract, please email me.
Regarding the noise, I consulted the landlord.

caution: に関して is the stiff written form of について — using it in everyday chat sounds overly formal.

peg.ja: その件に関して、私に発言権はない。
peg.romaji: Sono ken ni kanshite, watashi ni hatsugen-ken wa nai.
peg.en: Regarding that matter, I have no right to speak.
peg.source: PSYCHO-PASS — 狡噛慎也

nuance: Adds a second item on top of a first — often escalating (not just A, but also B).

examples.en: In addition to the rent, I also have to pay the utility bills.
Today, in addition to the wind, the rain is heavy too.
In addition to experience, Japanese ability is also required.

caution: に加えて attaches to a NOUN; to add a whole clause ("on top of doing X") you need 上に instead.

peg.ja: 実力に加えて、あいつには運もある。
peg.romaji: Jitsuryoku ni kuwaete, aitsu ni wa un mo aru.
peg.en: In addition to skill, he even has luck.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! — 及川徹

nuance: The result runs against an expectation, prediction, or rule. Formal; noun is usually 予想・期待・予測.

examples.en: Contrary to my expectations, the interview went well.
Contrary to the forecast, it was sunny all day.
Contrary to expectations, the rent negotiation went well.

caution: に反して needs an expectation-type noun (予想・期待) as its result-defying counterpart; don't confuse it with に沿って ("in line with"), its opposite.

peg.ja: 予想に反して、敵は退かなかった。
peg.romaji: Yosō ni hanshite, teki wa shirizokanakatta.
peg.en: Contrary to expectations, the enemy did not retreat.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

nuance: Acts on the basis of a rule, data, or principle — the noun is the foundation the action rests on.

examples.en: I pay the rent based on the contract.
Based on the survey results, we changed the menu.
Based on the rules, I sort the garbage.

caution: に基づいて means acting ON THE BASIS of a rule/data (the foundation); don't confuse it with によって ("by means of / depending on").

peg.ja: 証拠に基づいて、犯人はお前だ。
peg.romaji: Shōko ni motozuite, hannin wa omae da.
peg.en: Based on the evidence, you are the culprit.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン — 江戸川コナン

nuance: The noun is the contested subject that people argue, compete, or negotiate over. News-register.

examples.en: Residents are debating over the construction of the new station.
Employees and the company are at odds over overtime pay.
Over the garbage rules, the residents held a discussion.

caution: をめぐって marks a contested topic people argue OVER; for a neutral topic of speech or thought use について instead.

peg.ja: その宝をめぐって、争いが始まった。
peg.romaji: Sono takara o megutte, arasoi ga hajimatta.
peg.en: A struggle began over that treasure.
peg.source: ハンター×ハンター — クロロ

nuance: Physical (along a river/road) or abstract (in line with a plan or wishes) — following the shape of the noun.

examples.en: I rode my bike along the river.
I'll make the plan in line with the company's policy.
Following the instructions, I filled in the documents.

caution: に沿って means "following along" a line, plan, or wishes; don't confuse it with に反して ("contrary to"), which is its opposite.

peg.ja: 計画に沿って、俺たちは動くだけだ。
peg.romaji: Keikaku ni sotte, oretachi wa ugoku dake da.
peg.en: In line with the plan, we simply move.
peg.source: デスノート — 夜神月

nuance: A written, formal 〜で marking the place, time, or field of an event. Common in announcements and academic prose.

examples.en: The onboarding ceremony will be held at the venue.
In modern Japan, the labor shortage is a problem.
The interview was held in the meeting room.

caution: において is a written, formal で; it marks place/time/field, not a means or instrument (that stays で).

peg.ja: この戦場において、油断は死を意味する。
peg.romaji: Kono senjō ni oite, yudan wa shi o imi suru.
peg.en: On this battlefield, carelessness means death.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — リヴァイ

nuance: The noun-modifying form of 〜において — attaches directly to a following noun (the situation in X).

examples.en: Life in Tokyo is convenient.
The use of English in the workplace is increasing.
First impressions in an interview are very important.

caution: における is the noun-modifying form and must attach directly to a following noun; use において when it modifies a verb clause instead.

peg.ja: 組織における裏切りは、許されない。
peg.romaji: Soshiki ni okeru uragiri wa, yurusarenai.
peg.en: Betrayal within the organization is unforgivable.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

nuance: Two senses: 'together with' (accompaniment) and 'as X happens, Y also changes' (parallel change).

examples.en: Together with my colleagues, I went to watch the fireworks.
As the times changed, ways of working changed too.
As the seasons shift, the town's scenery changes too.

caution: とともに has an accompaniment sense and a parallel-change sense; for a purely proportional change, formal writing prefers に伴って.

peg.ja: 時の流れとともに、真実は明らかになる。
peg.romaji: Toki no nagare to tomo ni, shinjitsu wa akiraka ni naru.
peg.en: As time flows on, the truth comes to light.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — ロイ・マスタング

nuance: A change in one thing brings a proportional change in another. More formal/written than とともに.

examples.en: As the population increases, rents are rising too.
Accompanying the move, an address change is required.
Along with the rise in temperature, the electricity bill increased too.

caution: に伴って needs a real change/progression in both clauses; don't use it for mere accompaniment ("together with"), where とともに fits.

peg.ja: 力の増大に伴って、代償も大きくなる。
peg.romaji: Chikara no zōdai ni tomonatte, daishō mo ōkiku naru.
peg.en: As the power grows, so does the price.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 — 五条悟

nuance: Covered in or riddled with something unpleasant — mud, dust, mistakes. Always negative.

examples.en: Right after the move, the room was full of cardboard boxes.
The documents I submitted were full of mistakes.
After getting off the packed train, I was covered in sweat.

caution: だらけ is always negative (mud, mistakes, blood); for a neutral "full of" use だらけ's cousin 〜がいっぱい or plural まみれ carefully — never for something pleasant.

peg.ja: 傷だらけでも、まだ立てる。
peg.romaji: Kizu darake demo, mada tateru.
peg.en: Covered in wounds, I can still stand.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — 緑谷出久

nuance: Leaving something in a state you should have undone — often careless (left the light on) or nonstop (stood the whole time).

examples.en: I went out and left the light on.
On the packed train I stood the whole hour.
I left the window open and went out.

caution: っぱなし attaches to a V-stem and implies neglect or nonstop continuation, not a neutral ongoing state — don't substitute it for ている.

peg.ja: 扉は開けっぱなしだ。追ってこい。
peg.romaji: Tobira wa akeppanashi da. Otte koi.
peg.en: The door's left wide open. Come after me.
peg.source: ワンピース — ルフィ

nuance: A soft, formal 'I can't / I'm not in a position to' — used to decline politely without a blunt 'no'.

examples.en: The staff are unable to answer that question.
I'm not in a position to decide this on my own.
Regarding that matter, I am unable to give an answer.

caution: かねる means "(politely) cannot" while かねない means "might well (badly happen)" — these opposite meanings are the classic N2 trap; don't swap them.

peg.ja: その願いは、承服いたしかねます。
peg.romaji: Sono negai wa, shōfuku itashi kanemasu.
peg.en: That request, I am unable to accept.
peg.source: 黒執事 — セバスチャン

nuance: A bad outcome is quite possible — 'could well end up …'. Only for negative, unwanted results.

examples.en: If you overdo it, you might well ruin your health.
If I oversleep, I could well be late for the interview.
If you're underprepared, you might well fail the interview.

caution: かねない (a bad outcome "might well happen") is the opposite of polite-refusal かねる ("cannot") — and it only takes undesirable results.

peg.ja: この一手は、全てを崩壊させかねない。
peg.romaji: Kono itte wa, subete o hōkai sase kanenai.
peg.en: This one move might well bring everything crashing down.
peg.source: デスノート — L

connection: V-stem + 得る/得ない(えない)
nuance: Marks something as possible in principle. Formal/written; the negative is 得ない (えない).

examples.en: An earthquake can happen at any time.
That plan is a fully possible scenario.
It's a problem that can happen to anyone.

caution: The affirmative 得る reads うる or える, but the negative is only 得ない (えない) — never *うない; this reading split trips learners up.

peg.ja: あらゆる可能性が、起こり得る。
peg.romaji: Arayuru kanōsei ga, okori uru.
peg.en: Every possibility can occur.
peg.source: デスノート — 夜神月

nuance: There's no possible method to do the action — you'd do it if you could, but no means exists.

examples.en: I don't know the number, so there's no way to contact them.
I don't know the street name, so there's no way to explain it.
Without the parts, there's no way to fix it.

caution: ようがない means "no method exists to do it," not "no need to" (ことはない) — the impossibility is about means, not necessity.

peg.ja: この結界は、破りようがない。
peg.romaji: Kono kekkai wa, yaburi yō ga nai.
peg.en: This barrier — there's no way to break it.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 — 五条悟

connection: N / plain form + に過ぎない
nuance: Plays something down as merely X and no bigger — just a trainee, just a guess.

examples.en: Right now I'm nothing more than a trainee.
That's nothing more than my own guess.
That's nothing more than an excuse.

caution: に過ぎない downplays ("nothing more than X"); don't confuse it with に違いない ("must certainly be"), which does the opposite.

peg.ja: お前など、駒に過ぎない。
peg.romaji: Omae nado, koma ni suginai.
peg.en: You are nothing more than a pawn.
peg.source: コードギアス — ルルーシュ

connection: plain form + とは限らない
nuance: Denies that a plausible generalization always holds — X isn't guaranteed just because it usually seems so.

examples.en: An expensive restaurant isn't necessarily always tasty.
Not every Japanese person can necessarily use polite speech perfectly.
A cheap room isn't necessarily bad.

caution: とは限らない denies that a generalization ALWAYS holds ("not necessarily"); it doesn't mean the statement is outright false.

peg.ja: 正義が、常に勝つとは限らない。
peg.romaji: Seigi ga, tsune ni katsu to wa kagiranai.
peg.en: Justice does not necessarily always win.
peg.source: PSYCHO-PASS — 槙島聖護

meaning: it's natural that / should / used to
nuance: States a general truth, social expectation, or fond recollection — the way things naturally are or were.

examples.en: Back in my student days I used to miss the last train a lot.
When you're in trouble, people should help one another.
When I was young, I used to play all night.

caution: ものだ blends three senses — general truth, past habit ("used to"), and exclamation — and only the past-tense たものだ gives nostalgia; don't read every ものだ as advice.

peg.ja: 人は、いつか死ぬものだ。
peg.romaji: Hito wa, itsuka shinu mono da.
peg.en: People are bound to die someday.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — マース・ヒューズ

meaning: as if I would ever! (strong denial)
nuance: A rhetorical refusal — there's no way I'd ever do that. Blunt and emphatic; ものか in casual speech, ものですか softened.

examples.en: As if I'd ever go to that crowded shop again!
As if I'd ever want to ride such a packed train every day!
As if I'd ever do this job again!

caution: ものか is a blunt rhetorical denial ("as if I would!"), not a real question — soften it to ものですか in polite speech.

peg.ja: こんな所で、倒れてなるものか。
peg.romaji: Konna tokoro de, taorete naru mono ka.
peg.en: As if I'd go down in a place like this!
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — 緑谷出久

nuance: Logical impossibility — given the facts, that outcome simply cannot be. Stronger and more reasoned than はずがない.

examples.en: There's no way I can read every kanji after just one year.
There's no way he'd forget the promise.
After practicing this much, there's no way I won't pass.

caution: わけがない is reasoned logical impossibility; はずがない is softer expectation-based denial — don't treat them as fully interchangeable.

peg.ja: あいつが、約束を忘れるわけがない。
peg.romaji: Aitsu ga, yakusoku o wasureru wake ga nai.
peg.en: There's no way he'd forget a promise.
peg.source: ワンピース — ナミ

connection: plain form / N + に決まっている
nuance: Speaker's confident conviction — it's obvious, of course it is. Subjective certainty, not proven fact.

examples.en: Tokyo in June is bound to be muggy.
If I don't book now it's definitely going to be fully booked.
Of course you're bound to be nervous on the first day.

caution: に決まっている is subjective conviction ("of course!"), not proven fact — don't use it for objective certainties where に違いない fits better.

peg.ja: 勝つに決まっている。
peg.romaji: Katsu ni kimatte iru.
peg.en: We're bound to win, obviously.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! — 日向翔陽

nuance: Only one option remains — resigned, there's nothing else for it.

examples.en: I missed the last train, so I have no choice but to walk home.
The trains stopped for the typhoon, so I have no choice but to wait at home.
It was full, so I have no choice but to find another share house.

caution: しかない ("no choice but to") attaches to a dictionary-form verb; don't confuse it with the noun+しかない ("only X") counting sense.

peg.ja: 前に進むしかない。
peg.romaji: Mae ni susumu shika nai.
peg.en: There's no choice but to move forward.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — エレン

connection: V-ない stem + ざるを得ない (する→せざる)
nuance: Formal, reluctant obligation — forced by circumstance to do it against one's will. Written/stiff register.

examples.en: Because of the typhoon, we cannot help but cancel the trip.
If the boss asks, I cannot help but work overtime.
Since it's the rule, I have no choice but to comply.

caution: The irregular する becomes せざるを得ない (not *しざる), and くる → こざるを得ない — these stem changes are the classic mistake.

peg.ja: 犠牲は、払わざるを得ない。
peg.romaji: Gisei wa, harawazaru o enai.
peg.en: Sacrifices cannot but be paid.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — エルヴィン

connection: V-ない stem + ずにはいられない
nuance: An impulse too strong to resist — you simply can't stop yourself from doing it.

examples.en: When I recall the Hokkaido sea, I can't help wanting to go back.
When the cherry blossoms bloom, I can't help taking photos.
Seeing this view, I can't help being moved.

caution: The negative stem uses ず, so する → せずにはいられない (not *しずには) — mind the same せ-stem trap as ざるを得ない.

peg.ja: この光景を見ては、涙せずにはいられない。
peg.romaji: Kono kōkei o mite wa, namida sezu ni wa irarenai.
peg.en: Seeing this sight, I can't help but weep.
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 — 煉獄杏寿郎

connection: V-て / adj-くて + ならない
nuance: A feeling wells up beyond control — typically worry, curiosity, or emotion, slightly literary.

examples.en: I can't help worrying about the result of my residence card renewal.
I miss my friends back home unbearably.
I can't stop worrying about the interview result.

caution: てならない is mostly for spontaneous feelings (worry, curiosity); for physical sensations/desires, てたまらない is the more natural pick.

peg.ja: あの日のことが、悔やまれてならない。
peg.romaji: Ano hi no koto ga, kuyamarete naranai.
peg.en: I can't help but regret what happened that day.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — ロイ・マスタング

connection: V-て / adj-くて + たまらない
nuance: A physical sensation or desire so intense it's unbearable — hot, sleepy, itchy, dying to do something.

examples.en: The rainy-season humidity is unbearably unpleasant.
After overtime I'm dying for a cold beer.
I'm dying to move into the new room soon.

caution: てたまらない fits physical sensations and desires; it can't take a plain verb like 食べる directly — it needs a feeling/sensation word.

peg.ja: 強くなりたくてたまらない。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoku naritakute tamaranai.
peg.en: I'm dying to get stronger.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — 緑谷出久

connection: V-て / adj-くて + しかたがない
nuance: A feeling or sensation is overwhelming and there's nothing to be done about it — everyday register, like てたまらない.

examples.en: Before my first interview I couldn't help feeling extremely nervous.
Everything about the new neighborhood is so novel I can't get enough of it.
I miss my family back home terribly.

caution: てしかたがない ("can't help feeling") is emotional/sensory; don't confuse it with 〜てもしかたがない ("no use doing"), a different resignation pattern.

peg.ja: 先が気になってしかたがない。
peg.romaji: Saki ga ki ni natte shikata ga nai.
peg.en: I can't help being curious what comes next.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン — 江戸川コナン

nuance: Direct advice or a strong recommendation — the best thing to do is X. Speaker telling listener what's wise.

examples.en: If you want to get good at Japanese, you should speak it every day.
On typhoon days, you shouldn't go out unnecessarily.
In an interview, you should speak clearly.

caution: Advice-giving ことだ ("you should") is distinct from nominalizing こと and from exclamatory ことだ — it only follows a dictionary or ない verb of action.

peg.ja: 強くなりたいなら、鍛えることだ。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoku naritai nara, kitaeru koto da.
peg.en: If you want to get strong, you should train.
peg.source: ドラゴンボール — 亀仙人

connection: question word + plain form + ことか
nuance: An emotional exclamation of degree — how much, how many times, how long. Paired with どんなに / 何度 / どれほど.

examples.en: How much I've grown over this one year!
How many times I got lost on this hilly road!
How many people helped me this year!

caution: ことか needs a question word (どんなに, 何度, どれほど) up front to work as an exclamation — without it the emotive force is lost.

peg.ja: この日を、どれほど待ちわびたことか。
peg.romaji: Kono hi o, dore hodo machiwabita koto ka.
peg.en: How long I've awaited this day!
peg.source: ジョジョの奇妙な冒険 — DIO

nuance: Front-loads the speaker's emotional reaction before the fact — to my surprise, to my delight, sadly. Fixed emotion words.

examples.en: To my surprise, my neighbor was also from Hokkaido.
To my regret, the fireworks festival was cancelled due to rain.
To my delight, the rent goes down next month.

caution: ことに only takes a fixed set of emotion words (驚いた, 嬉しい, 残念な) and states the reaction FIRST — a neutral adjective can't slot in.

peg.ja: 驚いたことに、奴はまだ生きていた。
peg.romaji: Odoroita koto ni, yatsu wa mada ikite ita.
peg.en: To my surprise, he was still alive.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 — アルミン

nuance: A hedged, reluctant yes via a double negative — it's not that I can't, i.e. I could, with reservations.

examples.en: It's not that I can't eat natto, but every morning would be too much.
It's not that I couldn't take the overtime, but I have plans tonight.
It's not that I can't go, but I'm tired today.

caution: ないことはない is a hedged, reluctant "yes" via double negative — read it as "it's possible, with reservations," not a plain affirmative.

peg.ja: 勝てないことはない。
peg.romaji: Katenai koto wa nai.
peg.en: It's not that we can't win.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! — 澤村大地

nuance: Literary negative — a firm resolve not to do (I'll never) or a negative guess (surely not). Stiff, written register.

examples.en: I resolve never to make the same mistake again.
With weather like this, the train probably won't be delayed.
At this rate, it probably won't even snow this winter.

caution: まい is one-directional: first-person = firm resolve NOT to, third-person = negative guess ("surely won't") — don't read the resolve sense onto someone else.

peg.ja: 二度と、同じ過ちは犯すまい。
peg.romaji: Nido to, onaji ayamachi wa okasu mai.
peg.en: Never again will I make the same mistake.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 — エドワード

connection: V-dict / N-の + 恐れがある
nuance: A formal warning of an undesirable possibility — used in news and official notices for hazards and bad outcomes.

examples.en: There is a risk the trains will stop due to the typhoon.
There is a risk the renewal will be delayed due to a flaw in the documents.
Due to heavy snow, there is a risk transit will be disrupted.

caution: 恐れがある warns only of undesirable possibilities (disasters, risks); it can't mark a neutral or hoped-for outcome the way かもしれない can.

peg.ja: このままでは、計画が露見する恐れがある。
peg.romaji: Kono mama de wa, keikaku ga roken suru osore ga aru.
peg.en: At this rate, there's a risk the plan will be exposed.
peg.source: デスノート — 夜神月

connection: plain form / N + にもかかわらず
nuance: Don't confuse with にかかわらず (regardless of, N1) — にもかかわらず marks that the outcome DEFIED the circumstance.

examples.en: Despite the typhoon, the Yamanote line was running.
Despite being busy, my colleague helped me move.
Despite the rain, the festival was crowded with people.

caution: にもかかわらず ("despite") is not にかかわらず ("regardless of") — the も makes it concessive, marking a defied circumstance, not an irrelevant one.

peg.ja: 満身創痍にもかかわらず、彼は立ち上がった。
peg.romaji: Manshin sōi ni mo kakawarazu, kare wa tachiagatta.
peg.en: Despite being covered in wounds, he rose to his feet.
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 — 竈門炭治郎
